commit.message:
install-mcp: classify_server_sensitivity — install-time never-lossy suggestion (#90)

First slice of increment 2. A pure helper that flags, at install time, whether a
server looks credential/personal (name + launch command vs policy.SENSITIVE_SERVER_RE),
so install-mcp can PROMPT to bake it into never_lossy_servers. A suggestion, not an
auto-decision — the operator adds innocuous-named stores (kb, sb-run) by hand. The
runtime floor (PR #89) enforces the pattern independently regardless.

Wiring into do_install's interactive flow (prompt + write never_lossy_servers) is
the next slice.

commit.message:
policy: server-level lossy exclusion — never-lossy servers forced lossless (#89)

The mandatory safety floor for lossy productization: on a never-lossy server
(credential/personal store), truncate/drop are structurally forbidden — forced
100% lossless — even if a rule marks a field lossy. Enforced in apply() on the
VERIFIED server identity (#83), not a tool-name match, so a mislabeled or renamed
rule cannot leak a credential payload through a lossy transform.

Two layers:
- Policy.never_lossy_servers — a per-server list, baked at install time by the
  sensitivity classifier (next increment); catches sensitive servers by identity,
  including ones whose names look innocuous (a personal KB, a launcher alias).
- SENSITIVE_SERVER_RE — a non-overridable name floor (secret/credential/vault/
  token/password/key/auth) that a policy typo cannot defeat.

Parallels the existing Rule.capture guard: a declarative security property, safe
by construction rather than by operator discipline. Lossy stays dark today (no
policy enables it); this lands the floor before it turns on.

commit.message:
release: tag-based versioning (hatch-vcs) + GitHub Release CI (#88)

Make the git tag the single source of version truth. hatch-vcs derives the
version from `git describe`, so pyproject, the package, and releases cannot
drift — the hand-synced literal (and its drift bug) is gone entirely. A build
between tags gets a dev version; a build on a tag resolves to that tag.

- pyproject: [tool.hatch.version] source = "vcs"; hatch-vcs build hook writes
  the resolved version to src/terse/_version.py.
- __init__.py: __version__ imports the generated _version.py, falling back to
  importlib.metadata (raw source tree) then a sentinel — never a literal.
- .github/workflows/release.yml: on a `v*` tag push, build sdist+wheel, assert
  the built version matches the tag, publish a GitHub Release with generated
  notes + artifacts. No PyPI (git-install-first; `terse` is taken on PyPI).
- tests.yml: fetch-depth: 0 (hatch-vcs needs full history) + SHA-pinned actions.
- _version.py gitignored; mypy override for the generated, sometimes-absent module.

The first tag (v0.1.0) is cut after merge, so the release workflow already
exists at the tagged commit.

commit.message:
version: single-source from __init__.py + add `terse --version` (#87)

Collapse the two hand-synced version declarations into one. pyproject now
declares `dynamic = ["version"]` and hatchling reads `__version__` from
src/terse/__init__.py via [tool.hatch.version]; the static `version = "0.0.1"`
is removed. Kills the drift bug where pyproject and the package could disagree.

Add a `--version` flag to the root CLI parser (was missing entirely): reads the
installed distribution via importlib.metadata, falling back to the package
`__version__` for source-tree runs (`python -m terse` in a checkout).

Tag-derived versioning (hatch-vcs, tag = version) and the release CI are the
deferred next step, where git tags will exist to derive from.

commit.message:
policy: per-rule "capture": false — never persist a tool's payloads (#85) (#86)

--capture-dir tees every raw payload to disk before compression, ungated by
policy, and --debug-log records embed the raw payload too. So there was no way
to say "compress this server, but never persist that tool" -- which is what
blocked wrapping a credential-returning server (secret-broker's
reveal_credential returns a plaintext value by design).

A rule may now carry "capture": false, gating BOTH disk sinks. Notably NOT
implied by "tiers": [] -- passthrough only stops compression/diff state; the
capture tee sits above the tier logic. The payload-free stats ledger still
counts a gated tool (sizes + decision, never content), and what the client
receives is byte-identical either way.

Parsing is strict: a non-bool raises at load rather than coercing. Every
wrong-typed value in Python is truthy (bool("false") is True), so a lax
coercion would silently re-enable the guard -- the one direction a typo must
never fail in.

The point is structural: "this tool's output must never hit disk" becomes a
declarative property of the policy -- surviving re-wraps, reviewable in one
place, and expressible under --config multiproxy (one process, one capture_dir,
so omitting the flag cannot exclude one peer) -- instead of an operator
remembering never to pass --capture-dir to one particular wrapper.

commit.message:
policy: make server-scoped rules match servers that don't self-prefix (#83) (#84)

A rule like `runecho.*` is scoping to a SERVER, but MCP tool names don't
inherently carry one: kb names its own tools `kb.read.search` (so `kb.*`
matched unaided), while runecho calls its tool plain `structure` -- so
`runecho.*` matched nothing, silently fell through to defaults, and nothing
said so. Whether a server-scoped glob worked depended entirely on whether the
downstream happened to self-prefix its tool names.

Policy.select/apply now take an optional `server` and try a synthesized
`{server}.{bare tool}` candidate first, so such a rule means the same thing
for every server. Additive: existing candidates are still tried in their
original order, and a self-prefixed tool is never double-qualified.

`proxy --server-name NAME` supplies it; install-mcp bakes it in from the MCP
config's own server name; multiproxy passes each peer's config name. It also
labels the stats ledger with the real server instead of the command basename
(kb behind secret-broker's sb-run labeled itself "sb-run").

Found by the #82 savings ledger's first production rows.

commit.message:
live savings ledger + terse stats: payload-free per-result records, default-on (#82)

The proxy appends one payload-free JSONL record per tool result (ts, server,
tool, decision, char + cl100k token sizes -- never content) to
$XDG_STATE_HOME/terse/stats.jsonl; new `terse stats [--since] [--json]`
aggregates total savings, diff hit-rate, and per-server/per-tool rows from
real sessions. ON by default (safe: no payload content; same fail-open
side-effect contract as capture/audit); `proxy --no-stats`/--stats-log and
`install-mcp --no-stats` opt out. Ledger rotates at 10MB and self-heals a
torn tail from a crashed writer. Multiproxy records each peer's config name.
tests/conftest.py isolates XDG_STATE_HOME so tests never touch the real
ledger.

commit.message:
codec hot-path fix (#79) + fluency package split (#78) (#80)

* codec hot path: one memoized canonicalization pass + shared MAX_DEPTH cap (#79)

dict_encode's counting and replacement walks called minify(node) at every
container level — O(n*depth) full re-serializations, twice over, on the live
proxy path. A single bottom-up pass now builds each container's minified form
once from its children's forms (memoized by id), byte-identical to json.dumps
(pinned by test, incl. non-str-key coercion). compress_with reuses the root
canon as its base. ~4x on deep payloads; wide ones are tokenizer-bound.

Depth: capture's _MAX_SHAPE_DEPTH=200 becomes the codec-wide transforms.MAX_DEPTH,
checked iteratively (exceeds_depth) at every boundary — policy.apply and the
proxy's diff path pass a too-deep payload through untouched (same contract as
the marker guard), measure_payload reports it non-applicable instead of
RecursionError-ing, and json.loads RecursionError (the 3.11 floor) is caught
alongside JSONDecodeError.

* split fluency.py into a fluency/ package, facade preserves every import (#78)

Pure move of the 958-line five-job module into questions.py (three generators +
text-diff's), scoring.py (programmatic checking), answerers.py (Answerer protocol
+ urllib backend), harnesses.py (plain/diff/soak/text-diff runners), pack.py
(offline packs + PRIMER). The package __init__ re-exports the entire old module
namespace — including the private helpers dropeval.py reuses by design and the
fluency.text_diff / fluency.compress attributes tests patch/call — so cli,
report, dropeval, and the whole test suite run unchanged (0 test edits).
Docs/report references to fluency.py updated to the package paths.

commit.message:
audit fixes: checked types + curated lint; install-mcp drift guard; strict policy keys; transport credential guard (#77)

Three fixes from the 2026-07-14 module-by-module audit.

1. Annotations become a CHECKED contract. [tool.ruff.lint] curated select
   (I/B/UP/C4/SIM/RET/PLE on top of the defaults) + a lenient [tool.mypy]
   baseline, both in CI. Burn-down: 114 ruff findings (96 auto), 17 mypy
   errors - several real: pump()'s src annotation claimed TextIO but receives
   an Iterator from HttpTransport; answer_retrieve indexed the drop store
   with a possibly-None handle; text_diff reused a loop variable across int/
   int|None types; an invalid 'type: ignore' comment in _emit_audit was
   silently not suppressing anything.

2. install-mcp drift guard. A re-wrap used to rebuild the entry purely from
   the stashed original, silently reverting hand-edits to the WRAPPED entry
   (reverted codegraph's node@22 env.PATH pin in production, 2026-07-13).
   wrap() now carries live non-terse-owned keys forward (command/args still
   rebuilt; url/headers never resurrected), do_install reports what it
   preserved, and the CLI prints it with the uninstall caveat. USAGE updated.

3. Hardening pair.
   - load_policy rejects unknown keys at every level ('_'-prefixed
     annotations exempt - the policy_gen convention). Immediately caught a
     latent specimen: a multiproxy test's policy file used 'rules' (schema
     says 'policies') and the old loader silently ignored it.
   - HttpTransport refuses credential-bearing headers (authorization/token/
     key/secret/cookie) over cleartext http to a non-loopback host - parity
     with fluency.openai_answerer's TLS guard. Loopback gateways unaffected.

476 tests pass (+5), ruff clean, mypy clean, live policy file verified
against the strict loader.

commit.message:
flip cross-call diffing to default-on; add --no-diff opt-outs (#76)

The validation program that kept --diff opt-in is complete — pair fluency
(4-model panel 100%), nested-record coverage (#72), and the drift soak (#75:
mechanical zero-drift at depth 300, behavioral depth-1..5 PASS on real corpus
chains). The default now matches the measured reality.

- Policy.diff defaults True; load_policy's doc.get("diff") default flips to
  match. An explicit policy-file "diff": false is still honored.
- proxy: tri-state CLI — --diff forces on (only useful against an opting-out
  policy file), new --no-diff forces off, neither keeps the policy value.
  Mutually exclusive, exit 2. multiproxy diff_override becomes Optional[bool]
  (None = leave each peer's policy alone) so --no-diff is proxy-wide too.
- install-mcp: diff param is tri-state — plain wraps write NO diff flag and
  inherit the proxy default; --diff/--no-diff bake an explicit override into
  the entry; keyframe-interval no longer requires --diff (but is dropped with
  --no-diff, where it would be dead weight).
- Tests: the two off-by-default pins become on-by-default + policy-false
  opt-out pins; install-mcp tri-state covered (None drops flags, False bakes
  --no-diff). 471 pass, ruff clean.
- Docs: README tier/Status, TECHNICAL diff bullet (opt-in rationale rewritten
  as history + the compaction residual keeps --no-diff as escape hatch),
  USAGE proxy/install-mcp/text-diff sections; also documents the stash
  hand-edit gotcha found during the #74 rollout.

Live smoke: flagless proxy diffs the repeat structure call (117,587 -> 205
chars); --no-diff sends the full form both times.

  (None = leave each peer's policy alone) so --no-diff is proxy-wide too.

  inherit the proxy default; --diff/--no-diff bake an explicit override into
  the entry; keyframe-interval no longer requires --diff (but is dropped with
  --no-diff, where it would be dead weight).

  opt-out pins; install-mcp tri-state covered (None drops flags, False bakes
  --no-diff). 471 pass, ruff clean.
- Docs: README tier/Status, TECHNICAL diff bullet (opt-in rationale rewritten
  as history + the compaction residual keeps --no-diff as escape hatch),
  USAGE proxy/install-mcp/text-diff sections; also documents the stash
  hand-edit gotcha found during the #74 rollout.

Live smoke: flagless proxy diffs the repeat structure call (117,587 -> 205
chars); --no-diff sends the full form both times.

commit.message:
diff drift soak: mechanical long-chain pytest + fluency --diff-soak depth eval (#75)

The #8/#20 follow-up gating any --diff default-flip: per-hop losslessness was
proven, but nothing exercised DRIFT — reconstruction and comprehension over
long chains of consecutive diffs.

Mechanical (tests/test_diff_soak.py): drives the real Interceptor with an
independent client-side reconstructor (ModelView, public decode fns only) and
asserts exactness at EVERY hop — 400 interleaved hops across three evolving
workloads (records / dict-map / text log) with error interludes and a mid-soak
reconnect; a 300-diff unbounded chain (interval 0); a 200-step text-only
chain. Keyframe cadence <=5 verified throughout. Zero drift.

Behavioral (fluency --diff-soak [--soak-depth K --soak-windows N]): builds
real depth-1..K chain windows from the corpus (every hop must admit a lossless
diff), scores final-state questions as (full anchor + k wires) vs full-terse
control, reports accuracy by depth + worst-model verdicts overall and at the
deepest depth (build_diff_soak_report).

Enabler: _flat_record_questions — fluency-local questions for single flat
record payloads (keys-count + deterministic scalar lookups), the keys-diff
surface the soak was blind to (kb payloads are per-block flat dicts). Same
pattern as #72's nested-record source; codec/tabularizer rules untouched.

Live result (loopback gateway, deepseek-v4-flash + glm-5.2, 3 trials,
~50 real corpus windows): overall worst gap -2% +/-1, depth-5 gap -1% +/-2 —
PASS at 5% tolerance, no depth-correlated drift.

471 tests pass (+8), ruff clean.

commit.message:
fluency: cover nested-record tools (structure) so proxy --diff is validated (#71) (#72)

* fluency: cover nested-record tools (structure) so proxy --diff is validated there (#71)

The diff-fluency gate generated zero questions for runecho.structure — a dict-map of
file records whose symbols[] are non-uniform (imports carry name/kind; functions also
carry line/hash), which the strict identical-keyset extractor skips. So the high-value
diff surface (72-96% cross-call overlap, the +31.7% savings) was unvalidated while the
harness still reported PASS on locate alone.

Add fluency-local _nested_record_group + _nested_questions: group-scoped
count/enumerate/lookup (+ aggregate when a numeric column is shared) over the columns
present in EVERY record, scoped to the first file ("...under files[\"a/x.py\"]..."), so
non-uniform lists are handled and ground truth stays well-posed. Preferred over the
uniform extractor for dict-map payloads to avoid an ambiguous unscoped count. Does NOT
touch the shared extract_records/_uniform_dict_list — the tabularizer, probe, and
drop-path logic (#47) still see the strict rule unchanged.

Verified: real structure payloads now generate 3 questions each; structure diff-fluency
runs and PASSES (deepseek-v4-flash, 0 regressions). 457 tests pass (5 new nested-path).

* fluency: require a unique id column for the nested lookup question (#71 review)

Code review caught that _nested_questions picked the lookup's addressing column by
most-distinct values, not full uniqueness. When that column has duplicates (common in
structure: repeated 'kind', overloaded 'name'), the prompt 'the record whose X is V'
matches multiple records but bakes in one arbitrary expected value — a truthful answer
about another matching record scores as a false-negative regression, exactly on the
structure payloads this feature enables.

Split the concerns: enumerate keeps using the most-distinct string column (order/count
check tolerates duplicates); lookup now reuses the uniform path's _pick_id_col (requires
len(set)==n) and is skipped entirely when no column uniquely addresses a record. Add a
regression test for the all-duplicate case.

commit.message:
chore: remove all Anthropic API integration (never used) (#73)

* chore: remove all Anthropic API integration (never used)

terse never used an Anthropic API key, so the anthropic code paths were dead weight
that implied a workflow we don't run. Remove them entirely:

- fluency.anthropic_answerer + dropeval.anthropic_tool_answerer (and the two
  _to_anthropic_* adapters) — the "real consumer" eval arm. The fluency eval reaches
  every model over the OpenAI-compatible path (broker pool or a loopback gateway).
- tokenize.count_anthropic + measure --anthropic — the count_tokens "ground truth"
  check. cl100k stays the estimate; cross-tokenizer (cl100k vs o200k) invariance is
  the keyless robustness check, unchanged.
- the --anthropic / --anthropic-model CLI flags, the Anthropic report section, the
  `anthropic` optional extra (pruned from uv.lock with its transitive deps), and the
  doc/env mentions.

No behavior change to any path terse actually uses. 452 tests pass; ruff clean.

* chore: stop tracking machine-local .codegraph index

Accidentally staged by git add -A in the previous commit; it is a local symlink
to the codegraph db, not repo content. Remove from tracking and gitignore it.

commit.message:
Add terse probe --cross-server: #64 Phase 0 cross-peer redundancy gate (#65)

* Add terse probe --cross-server: #64 Phase 0 cross-peer redundancy gate

Measures whether co-resident MCP servers (kb + codegraph + runecho) share
enough repeated content that a shared cross-peer dictionary would beat the
per-peer compression terse already does — the go/no-go gate for #64 Phase 1.
Measurement only; compresses nothing.

Two levers, reusing the existing probe machinery across server boundaries:

- Lever A (value dictionary): pooled-vs-per-peer est_dict_saving increment
  over extracted record cells. Isolates values repeated across >=2 servers.
- Lever B (framing-normalized overlap): idf-weighted cross-server raw-token
  overlap. Weighting by inverse document frequency drops ubiquitous JSON
  framing tokens, so only shared CONTENT counts — a shape-agnostic signal
  that spans text/source servers Lever A can't see.

Server identity is reverse-mapped from the captured tool name (the capture
envelope stores only `tool`; the corpus was captured by separate per-server
proxies sharing one --capture-dir).

The report carries a coverage guard: when a server present in the corpus
produces no record-shaped payloads, Lever A is flagged blind and the verdict
falls back to Lever B's framing-netted content overlap rather than emitting a
false "close".

Run on the real 770-payload corpus: Lever A blind to codegraph (0 record
payloads); Lever B content overlap 8.2% (raw 14.8%). Verdict INCONCLUSIVE —
the corpus is cross-session so 8.2% is a floor; a co-resident capture is the
decisive follow-up.

424 tests pass.

* Fix cross-server probe: gate on raw payloads so Lever B runs on text/source corpora

The --cross-server gate required >=2 servers with RECORD-shaped payloads, which
wrongly aborted the whole probe when servers emit text/source (codegraph) or
pre-compressed output — exactly the case Lever B (framing-normalized overlap)
exists to measure. Gate on raw payloads instead; Lever A degrades to empty and
the report's coverage guard flags it blind while Lever B carries the verdict.

Also: cross_server_redundancy now handles an empty record set without dividing
by zero, and the report's coverage-gap + verdict wording is coherent when Lever
A is fully empty (previously rendered "measured only across ." and told the user
to "confirm with a co-resident capture" even when the corpus already was one).

Validated on a co-resident capture (kb + codegraph + runecho about the same
terse symbols): framing-netted content overlap 20.9% vs the 8.2% cross-session
floor — a 2.5x jump, verdict LEAN BUILD. codegraph is the hub (up to 54% content
overlap with runecho; 20-36% with kb).

425 tests pass.

commit.message:
Add terse fluency --text-diff-eval: behavioral gate for text-diff codec (#63)

* Add terse fluency --text-diff-eval: behavioral gate for the text-diff codec

text_diff.py (Tier 0.7, non-JSON tool output) had a round-trip correctness
gate but no proof a model can actually use the diff form, unlike the JSON
diff which has --diff. Reuses run_diff_payload's paired-comparison design
and diff_gap_rows/build_terminal_diff_report unchanged; build_diff_report
is split into a shared _build_diff_style_report helper (pinned by a
regression test) so build_text_diff_report can label its control column
"raw text" instead of "full-terse" (Tier 0 doesn't compress non-JSON text).

* Fix code-review findings on text-diff-eval: dup wire computation, scoring gap, coverage

- run_text_diff_payload computed text_diff_wire twice per comparison (once via
  gen_text_diff_questions's gate, once to build diff_data); now computed once.
- The last-line question could have expected="", colliding with _safe_ask's
  empty-string return on answerer failure and scoring a crash as correct; those
  questions are now skipped when the target line is empty.
- Pairing filter only checked curr for JSON-shape, not prev, and used a bare
  json.loads/except that misses RecursionError on deeply-nested input; now uses
  capture.classify_shape (checked on both sides) like measure.py already does.
- Added a mid-document question so the eval also stresses reconstruction of
  unchanged (referenced) chunks, not just the edited tail.
- _build_diff_style_report's empty-hint collapsed build_diff_report's original
  two-line wrap into one line; empty_hint is now pre-split per caller so
  build_diff_report's output stays byte-identical to before the refactor.
- Extracted the pairing/aggregation scaffold shared by run_diff_fluency and
  run_text_diff_fluency into _iter_consecutive_pairs/_aggregate_by_model.

commit.message:
install-mcp: support project (.mcp.json) and local (nested projects.<path>) scope (#59)

Extends install-mcp/uninstall-mcp with --scope {user,project,local} (default
user, preserving current behavior). Local scope's default --repo-path resolves
via `git rev-parse --git-common-dir`, so a claudew/codexw bare-worktree repo
shares one local-scope entry across all its worktrees instead of the wrong
per-worktree cwd. The sidecar stash is now namespaced per scope so the same
server can be independently managed in more than one scope at once, with
transparent migration of the pre-existing flat stash format.

Closes #58

commit.message:
fix: address code-review findings on HTTP/SSE transport + multi-peer fan-out (#57)

* fix: address code-review findings on HTTP/SSE transport + multi-peer fan-out

Fixes all 19 confirmed/plausible findings from the high-effort review of this
branch (posted as a PR comment): 10 correctness bugs, 3 efficiency issues, and
6 cleanup/duplication items.

Correctness:
- report.py/VERIFY.md: the "no egress" claim was stale — HttpTransport now
  makes real outbound calls as a core feature, not just fluency's opt-in eval.
- multiproxy._build_peers: only caught OSError, so a later peer's malformed
  policy file (ValueError from load_policy) orphaned an earlier peer's
  already-launched subprocess.
- cli.py/multiproxy: --diff/--diff-keyframe-interval CLI overrides only
  reached default_policy, never a peer's own policy_path — silently, with no
  error, unlike the --header+--config case which is explicitly rejected.
- multiproxy.Router._route_call: routed (non-broadcast) tools/call had no
  timeout, unlike _broadcast's BROADCAST_TIMEOUT — a hung/dead peer left the
  client's request unanswered forever.
- proxy.Interceptor/multiproxy._broadcast: a broadcast-rewritten initialize's
  reply is swallowed before transform_response's one-time init_id reset ever
  runs, leaving init_id permanently stale.
- multiproxy._route_call/capture: peer identity was stripped before capture/
  audit, so same-named tools on different peers collided into one corpus
  bucket. Interceptor.pending now tracks (policy_tool, capture_tool) so
  policy/compression still uses the bare name while capture/audit see the
  peer-qualified one.
- proxy.Interceptor: the shared multiproxy lock covered transform_response's
  entire body (compression + capture/audit disk I/O), serializing every peer
  behind one slow peer's work. Split into a private _local_lock (pending/
  init_id/last/since_keyframe) and a shared _store_lock (drop store only).
- transport.HttpTransport._post: a 4xx/5xx error discarded the real
  downstream error body; now reads it, forwards a real JSON-RPC error object
  verbatim when the downstream sent one, and includes the raw body text in
  terse's own message otherwise. A failed notification (no id) no longer
  synthesizes a spurious id:null reply.
- proxy.Interceptor.transform_response: an error-shaped reply for a tracked
  id left its `pending` entry unpopped until PENDING_MAX eviction.
- multiproxy._merge_initialize: iterated peers by config index, not arrival
  order, contradicting its own "first-arriving peer's" documented contract.
- transport.Transport: added half_close()/wait() so run_proxy/multiproxy no
  longer reach through the Protocol into StdioTransport-only `.proc`, and no
  longer duplicate the same isinstance-driven teardown logic in two files.

Efficiency:
- dropeval.py: gen_drop_questions and run_drop_payload each independently
  ran a full policy.apply() pass over the same payload; now share one
  _questions_and_staging() call.
- dropeval.run_drop_fluency: loop nesting was model-outer, redoing the JSON
  parse + policy.apply() for every configured model; now envelope-outer, so
  that work happens once per payload regardless of model count.

Cleanup:
- cli.py: merged _build_answerers/_build_tool_answerers into one function
  parameterized by the answerer factory pair.
- proxy.py/multiproxy.py: extracted _build_capture_and_audit and
  _install_sigterm_to_exit/_ignore_sigterm/_restore_sigterm — both were
  copy-pasted near-verbatim between run_proxy and run_multi_proxy.
- cli.py: install-mcp's before/after display now handles url/headers-shaped
  entries instead of printing "before: ?".
- fluency --drop-eval --bars now renders a three-metric terminal forest plot
  (retrieve-recall, no-overfetch, final-accuracy) instead of silently doing
  nothing.

273 new/updated test assertions across 8 test files. Full suite (349 tests)
green on Python 3.11 and the dev interpreter; ruff clean.

* fix: close routed-call timeout race, id collision, and eviction gaps in multiproxy

Fixes findings from a high-effort code review of this PR's own diff:

- Router._route_call now registers the timeout timer before writing to the
  peer (was after), closing a race where a fast reply could leave an orphaned
  timer that later fires a spurious timeout at an already-answered client; a
  reused id's prior timer is now cancelled instead of silently overwritten.
- from_peer now identifies a peer-initiated server request (sampling/roots)
  before touching _routed_timers, so its unnamespaced id can no longer
  collide with an unrelated in-flight routed call's timeout bookkeeping.
- _routed_timed_out eviction is now primarily age-based (a TTL tied to
  broadcast_timeout) with a much larger population backstop, so a burst of
  concurrent timeouts no longer prematurely evicts an id still plausibly
  awaiting its own late reply and double-answering the client.
- Added Router.drain_routed_calls(), mirroring drain_pending_broadcasts, so
  an in-flight routed call gets its full timeout window before shutdown
  instead of being cut short.
- Policy.select now falls back to the bare tool name for a multiproxy
  peer-qualified one (e.g. "gh__search" -> "search"), fixing fluency
  --drop-eval silently reporting zero signal for any corpus captured
  through multiproxy.
- HttpTransport._post's JSON-RPC-shaped error forwarding now checks for a
  notification (no id) before forwarding, matching the sibling path; folded
  the id/has-id parse into one shared helper along the way.
- Corrected VERIFY.md/report.py's egress-audit claim, which named the wrong
  file list for its own grep command and omitted dropeval.py's live network
  calls entirely.
- Documented the Interceptor lock-ordering invariant more prominently (no
  live bug, but nothing previously enforced it).

349 -> 355 tests; ruff clean.

commit.message:
HTTP/SSE transport, multi-peer fan-out, and drop-to-retrieve eval (#5, #10) (#55)

* feat: HTTP/SSE transport, multi-peer fan-out via --config, and drop-to-retrieve behavioral eval (#5, #10)

Adds a Transport abstraction (stdio unchanged, plus MCP Streamable-HTTP) so
proxy.pump() and Interceptor never need to know how the downstream is
reached; multiproxy.py fronts N downstream peers (mixed stdio/HTTP) behind
one process with tool-name prefixing and a shared drop-to-retrieve store;
dropeval.py adds a 2-turn tool-loop harness for `fluency --drop-eval` to
measure whether a real tool-calling model actually calls terse.retrieve
when a dropped field matters.

* fix: multi-peer proxy correctness bugs from code review (broadcast id collisions, misrouted server requests, orphaned peers, shared drop-store bounds)

Fixes 10 findings from a /code-review high pass on the #5/#10 diff: broadcast
bookkeeping keyed by client id let a reused/late id leak an unmerged reply or
corrupt a concurrent broadcast; server-initiated requests from a non-zero peer
were misdelivered to peer 0; a partially-launched peer set orphaned its child
on a later launch failure; Interceptor's drop-store byte cap stayed
per-instance even when the store was shared across peers; the client->server
fan-out blocked all peers behind one slow HTTP send; a bad --policy path
crashed before the missing-command check; --header was silently dropped with
--config; peer names could collide under the tool-prefix separator; and the
peer-0-only fallback note was hidden without --debug. Broadcasts now use a
Router-local sequence number instead of the client's id, server-initiated
request ids are rewritten and tracked back to their origin peer, and each
peer gets its own background sender thread. Adds regression tests for each.

commit.message:
feat: charted HTML report mode for measure/verify (#52)

* feat: charted HTML report mode for measure/verify (#51)

Adds html_report.py — a zero-JS, zero-CDN inline-SVG companion to report.py's
markdown: diverging bars for savings %, stacked bars for tier attribution, and
a forest plot for per-model fluency/diff comprehension gaps. Reuses report.py's
stats math so the verdict never diverges from the markdown. Wired via --html
on `terse measure`/`terse verify`, writing next to --out with a .html suffix.

Palette validated against the project's data-viz method (light/dark, both pass
all six categorical checks); rendered against real measured data and screenshot
-checked in both modes for layout issues before wiring into the CLI.

* fix: don't clamp negative tiers in the HTML tier-attribution chart

stacked_bar_chart drew each tier segment as max(v, 0), silently rendering a
negative tier as zero-width — but measure.py is explicit that tier savings
can go negative ("at tiny N the table envelope can cost more than the keys
it folds") and report.py's markdown table already prints that correctly.
The chart's visual width could disagree with its own printed total.

Redraws it as a waterfall of cumulative steps instead: a negative step
retreats left of its predecessor (never clamped), gets a critical-red
outline + reduced opacity to flag it as the anomaly it is, and carries a
direct label — except where that label would be too cramped or collide
with a sibling label, in which case it defers to the always-present hover
title and table view rather than overlapping text (marks-and-anatomy.md's
"measure first, don't clip" rule).

stacked_bar_chart drew each tier segment as max(v, 0), silently rendering a
negative tier as zero-width — but measure.py is explicit that tier savings
can go negative ("at tiny N the table envelope can cost more than the keys
it folds") and report.py's markdown table already prints that correctly.
The chart's visual width could disagree with its own printed total.

Redraws it as a waterfall of cumulative steps instead: a negative step
retreats left of its predecessor (never clamped), gets a critical-red
outline + reduced opacity to flag it as the anomaly it is, and carries a
direct label — except where that label would be too cramped or collide
with a sibling label, in which case it defers to the always-present hover
title and table view rather than overlapping text (marks-and-anatomy.md's
"measure first, don't clip" rule).
